Military Mar 29, 2026

Rare US "Eye In The Sky" Command Plane Destroyed In Iran Strike On Saudi Air Base

The US has just a handful of E-3 Sentry's in mission-capable condition.

Loss of critical AWACS capability reduces US air surveillance redundancy in Middle East operations, straining a fleet already below optimal readiness levels.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 29, 2026

USAF E-3 Sentry AWACS damaged in Iranian strike on Saudi air base

An Iranian missile and drone attack on Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia on March 27, 2026,…...

The vulnerability of high-value AWACS platforms to coordinated missile-drone attacks signals a critical need for air defense modernization across coalition partners in the Middle East.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Just 2 Hours: The World's Shortest Airbus A380 Route Gets Even More Flights

Two different carriers fly their superjumbos on this corridor.

Competing carriers are doubling down on A380 operations on the world's shortest superjumbo route, signaling continued profitability in high-capacity short-haul markets despite fuel costs.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Regulatory Mar 29, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

The SMS Declaration of Compliance requires operators to formally certify their safety management systems meet FAA standards, creating documented accountability for systematic risk management rather than reactive incident response.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

7 Longest Nonstop Flights In The World In 2026

Four out of the seven use the Boeing 787.

Boeing 787's fuel efficiency and range capabilities dominate ultra-long-haul operations, giving airlines competitive advantages on transcontinental routes.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Mar 29, 2026

‘Shots on goal and win the game’: NASA’s effort to accelerate lunar landings

While NASA outlined plans to increase the cadence of robotic lunar lander missions at a recent event, it said little about accelerating work on crewed lunar landers.

NASA's focus on accelerating robotic lunar missions may create technology and operational pathways that could eventually benefit crewed landing timelines, though the agency remains non-committal on direct acceleration of human lander development.

See SpaceNews →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

"The Runway Wasn't Clear": Southwest 737 Touches Down Before Abrupt Burbank Go-Around

Reportedly, the pilot announced to the passengers that the runway was not clear when the aircraft touched down, resulting in a go-around.

Runway incursion risks persist even at major airports, requiring enhanced ground coordination protocols between ATC and flight crews during landing sequences.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective safety risk management requires organizations to systematically document why certain residual risks remain acceptable after implementing feasible controls, creating defensible compliance records.

Military Mar 29, 2026

USAF A-10 strike on Iraqi military base kills seven soldiers

An airstrike attributed to a US Air Force A-10 Thunderbolt II hit a military clinic at the Habbaniyah…...

Close air support operations in contested airspace require enhanced target verification protocols to minimize unintended casualties among allied forces.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Do Airlines Make Money From Economy Seats?

This heavily depends on the carrier and its operations.

Economy seat profitability varies drastically by airline model, with ultra-low-cost carriers relying on high-density seating while legacy carriers cross-subsidize economy losses with premium cabin revenue.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Alaska Airlines Pilots Ask For Police “To Meet The Airplane” After Passenger Vapes Onboard

An Alaska Airlines flight from Seattle to Sacramento had to request police to meet the aircraft at the gate after a passenger allegedly vaped.

In-flight disruptions from vaping enforcement are now escalating to law enforcement interventions, signaling growing operational friction around nicotine regulations at cruise altitude.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Safety Mar 29, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strikes remain a top-three fatal helicopter cause despite pilots' overconfidence in their reaction capability, indicating a critical gap between perceived and actual hazard response time.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Safety Mar 29, 2026

I’m A Flight Attendant: This Is Why Boarding Is The Most Stressful Part Of The Job

A lot of responsibility in a short time.

Boarding's compressed timeline forces flight attendants to simultaneously manage safety protocols, passenger seating, and customer service under extreme time pressure—a critical operational vulnerability airlines should address through process redesign.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 29, 2026

6 Military Aircraft With The Most Complex Avionics

The flying supercomputers of 2026.

Modern military aircraft avionics integration now requires specialized pilot training and maintenance protocols that commercial operators should monitor for potential civilian application spillover.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Why Do Airlines Keep Older Aircraft In Service Longer Than Expected?

Older aircraft come with higher operating costs, but have unique capabilities and have already been paid off by their operator.

Fully amortized older aircraft generate higher profit margins despite elevated maintenance costs, making economic retirement timelines longer than technological obsolescence would suggest.

See Simple Flying →

▶
Regulatory Mar 29, 2026

KSQL Noise Abatement Departure

The San Francisco Bay area is complicated airspace, and right in the middle of it is San Carlos Airport. San Carlos airport is surrounded by terrain, San Francisco International class bravo,...

San Carlos Airport's noise abatement procedures require pilots to navigate complex Bay Area airspace while managing terrain constraints and proximity to SFO's Class B airspace.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

JetBlue Passenger With Highest Mosaic Status Caught Performing Inappropriate Act Inflight

The ACARS message sent during JetBlue Flight 2858 revealed a shocking act taking place in the cabin, involving a passenger in Mint.

High-value frequent flyer status provides no exemption from inflight misconduct enforcement, underscoring airlines' obligation to maintain cabin discipline regardless of passenger tier.

See Simple Flying →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Northrop Grumman Testbed With AIM-260-Like Nose Spotted Flying Alongside Raytheon’s VOODOO 1 at Eglin AFB

The two testbeds, known by their callsigns SCAN 06 and VOODOO 1, carried out concurrent operations in Eglin’s highly instrumented airspace. For three consecutive days, on Mar. 24, 25 and 26, two testbed aircraft, the Northrop Grumman CRJ-700 carrying the U.S. registration code N806X and typically...

Concurrent testing of advanced air-to-air missile systems by competing contractors suggests accelerated development timelines for next-generation air combat capabilities.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Starfish Space finds a new partner for docking demonstration mission

Starfish Space has changed plans for a docking demonstration mission after its original partner backed out.

Starfish Space's pivot to a new docking partner underscores the critical importance of reliable collaboration in on-orbit servicing, a capability essential for future satellite constellation management.

See SpaceNews →

Industry Mar 29, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across integrated airline-airport-flight systems offers a near-term pathway to environmental gains without waiting for next-generation aircraft technology.

See MDPI →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Germany Considering MQ-28A Ghost Bat CCA, Says Defense Minister

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius said the MQ-28 Ghost Bat is among the uncrewed aircraft being evaluated to support the Eurofighter Typhoon fighter fleet. Germany’s defense minister Boris Pistorius said during his visit to Australia on Mar. 27, 2026, that Berlin is considering Boeing Defence...

Germany's evaluation of the MQ-28 Ghost Bat represents a strategic shift toward integrating AI-enabled collaborative combat aircraft with existing fighter fleets across NATO allies.

See The Aviationist →

Military Mar 28, 2026

USMC F-35Cs and USS Tripoli ARG Arrive in the Middle East

A month after the start of the hostilities, the U.S. Marine Corps is increasing its presence in the Middle East with the arrival of a F-35C squadron and the USS Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group. Mar. 28, 2026, marks a month since the U.S. and Israel launched Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion,...

USMC F-35C carrier operations in the Middle East signal escalated naval aviation capability deployment for sustained regional power projection.

See The Aviationist →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Robinson Unmanned

Robinson Unmanned (formerly Ascent AeroSystems) is the uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) business unit of Robinson...

Robinson's UAS division represents traditional helicopter manufacturers' strategic pivot toward autonomous systems to capture growing commercial drone markets.

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Quantum Systems Strengthens Ukraine’s Air Defence Capabilities with 15,000 Interceptor Contract and WIY Drones Investment

In alignment with the defence priorities set out by the Ukrainian Government, Quantum Systems has...

German drone manufacturer Quantum Systems is expanding its operational footprint in Ukraine through both strategic procurement contracts and local manufacturing investments, signaling manufacturer confidence in sustained demand for tactical UAS.

See sUAS News →

International Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF levy rollout amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Geopolitical tensions affecting oil prices are causing airlines to postpone sustainable aviation fuel investments, signaling that fuel cost volatility remains a primary barrier to SAF adoption.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

The crew's arrival signals NASA's readiness to execute the first crewed lunar mission since Apollo, with launch windows now measured in days rather than months.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers directly impacts U.S. air operations' refueling capacity and combat effectiveness across the Middle East region.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

NASA's lunar communications infrastructure relies on specialists like Richards whose decades of deep-space network expertise directly enable crewed mission success and future lunar operations.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M contract with Intuitive Machines expands commercial partnerships to support human lunar missions, reducing agency costs while advancing Artemis infrastructure development.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards framework increasingly integrates sustainability criteria into aerospace operations, signaling industry-wide pressure to align space programs with climate objectives.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen engine parts from scrapped aircraft may be fraudulently reintroduced into supply chains with falsified documentation, requiring enhanced verification protocols during maintenance acquisitions.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

CSF's expansion into lunar mobility and extreme-environment power solutions signals industry convergence toward sustained cislunar operations requiring integrated infrastructure.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

Europe's delayed Gateway decision creates uncertainty for space industry suppliers and lunar mission timelines as NASA's pivot away from the orbital station forces ESA to reassess investment priorities.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke Air Force Base's air show demonstrates the F-35's operational presence and modernization priorities within active USAF squadrons to civilian audiences.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band SAR penetrates cloud cover for Earth observation, providing all-weather geospatial intelligence critical for aviation weather modeling and terrain mapping.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band SAR capability to penetrate cloud cover enables continuous volcanic and geological monitoring critical for aerospace earth observation missions.

See NASA →
